title: Electoral Pluralism Index
subtitle: Data by the [Economist Intelligence Unit](#dod:economist-intelligence-unit). Expert estimates of the extent to which all citizens can choose their political leaders in free and fair elections. Higher values indicate more pluralism.
caption:
Electoral Pluralism Index, 2024
Data by the Economist Intelligence Unit. Expert estimates of the extent to which all citizens can choose their political leaders in free and fair elections. Higher values indicate more pluralism.

The world map displays the Electoral Pluralism Index for 2024, using a continuous color scale from deep red (lowest pluralism, near 1) to deep blue (highest pluralism, near 10) to show how much citizens in each country can choose political leaders in free and fair elections. The highest values cluster across western and northern Europe — including Norway, Sweden, Finland, Denmark, the Netherlands, Germany, and the United Kingdom — as well as Canada, Australia, New Zealand, Japan, South Korea, and most of Latin America such as Brazil, Argentina, Chile, and Mexico. The United States and India fall in the middle bands, with colors corresponding to the 6–8 range. The lowest values appear across much of sub-Saharan Africa — including Sudan, Ethiopia, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and Zimbabwe — as well as across the Middle East and North Africa, where countries like Saudi Arabia, Iran, Egypt, and Syria are shaded in the darkest red bands. Russia and China also sit in the lowest category, colored in deep red. Greenland and a few small island territories are marked with diagonal hatching to indicate no data. The figures exclude international aviation and other non-resident populations, as the index measures conditions for citizens within each territory. Across continents, the distribution shows a clear geographic pattern: high pluralism concentrated in established democracies of Europe, Oceania, and parts of the Americas; low pluralism dominant in authoritarian or conflict-affected states across Eurasia and Africa; and intermediate values scattered across South Asia, Southeast Asia, and parts of Latin America. The most visible pattern is the sharp contrast between the deep blue of western Europe and the deep red stretching from North Africa through Central Asia.
